questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:The children as well as their mother _ missing after the floods.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it uses the singular verb "is" which doesn't match the plural subject.

This option is correct because it uses the plural verb "are" which matches the plural subject "The children... and their mother", which fits the context of the sentence.Subject-verb agreement: Use a plural verb (e.g, "are") with plural subject (e.g, The childern)
This option is incorrect because it uses the singular verb "was" which doesn't match the plural subject "The children... and their mother".
This option is incorrect because it is a negative form of the verb "were" which doesn't fit the context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: Here there is a mention of more than one child hence it is plural and hence a plural verb "are" should be used.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:If you _ (start) at once you can reach by 6 o'clock.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is correct because it uses the base form of the verb "start", which is correct for the second person singular (you) in the present tense, especially with the conditional "if".Conditional sentences: Use the present tense in the "if" clause to describe a hypothetical situation.
This option is incorrect because it uses the present participle form of the verb "start" which doesn't fit in this sentence. .
This option is incorrect because it uses the third person singular present tense form of the verb "start" which doesn't match the subject "you".

This option is incorrect because it uses the present perfect tense which indicates an action that started in the past and continues up to the present moment It doesn't fit the conditional sentence's context.

questions.explanation: Option A is the most appropriate tense of the verb as it correctly links itself with the subject of the sentence.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:The gardner will have _ flowers.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option uses the past participle form of the verb "pluck", which is correct for describing an action that will be completed at a specific point in the future (using "will have").
This option is incorrect because it uses the base form of the verb "pluck", which doesn't fit in this sentence. The base form is used for present tense .
This option is incorrect because it uses the present tense form of the verb "pluck", which doesn't match the future perfect tense context of the sentence.

options.text: None of the above options is correct
options.explanation: Option A is correct the verb "pluck", which is correct for describing an action that will be completed at a specific point in the future (using "will have").

questions.explanation: "Will have" indicates something that would have happened in the past.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct option:By next Saturday, I _ (work) on this painting for exactly one month.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the correct option because it uses the future perfect continuous tense indicating an action that will be ongoing for a specific duration (one month) up to a specific point in the future (next Saturday).
This option uses the future continuous tense indicating an action that will be happening at a specific point in the future.
This option uses the future tense indicating an action that will happen at a specific point in the future.
This option is incorrect because it uses the past tense indicating an action that happened at a specific point in the past.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct, which is a future perfect tense. The future perfect tense refers to a completed action in the future.

questions.text: Choose the correct option according to subject verb agreementIt_ (rain) since last night.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option does not have the helping verb, hence it is incorrect.
As this option has both the helping verb and the ‘ing’ form of verb (present participle), this option is correct.
This option uses the first form of the verb, so it is incorrect.
This option uses the second form of the verb, so it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: "Since" in the sentence indicates the present perfect tense. In the present perfect tense, we use the helping verb has/have been + the ing form of the verb (present participle).

questions.text: Which of the following sentences is correct?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Last year I go to India.
options.explanation: This is the incorrect option go is used which is the 1st form of the verb. This is used to indicate the present tense.

options.text: Last year I went to India.
options.explanation: Went is the 2nd form of the verb go used in the past tense hence this is the correct option.

options.text: Last year I am going to India.
options.explanation: This is the incorrect option because the Auxiliary verb used with the ing form of the verb indicates a continuous tense which in this case is the present continuous tense.

options.text: Last year I will go to India.
options.explanation: Will/Shall + 1st form of the verb is used in the simple future tense. With the words "Last year" given in the sentence the action taking place in the future doesn’t make any sense.

questions.explanation: To identify the answer, we first need to identify the keyword which is "Last year". This indicates that the speaker performed the action in the past, hence past tense should be used. So this sentence is correct. Went the 2nd form of the verb go used in the past tense hence this is the correct option.

questions.text: Demonstrate correct use of subject-verb agreement:They _ a letter.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Wrote" is the past tense of the verb "write."We use it when we talk about something someone did already — an action that happened in the past."They wrote a letter."This means they already finished writing the letter — it happened in the past.
Written Past participle of "write", usually used with has/have/had.Example: "They have written a letter."Why it’s wrong here: On its own, "They written a letter" is grammatically incorrect.

Write Base form, used with plural subjects in present tense.Example: "They write letters every day."Why it’s wrong here: The sentence seems to be in past tense, so "write" doesn’t fit unless we change the whole context.
Writes Present tense, used with he/she/it.Example: "He writes a letter every week."Why it’s wrong here: "They" is plural, so "writes" doesn’t match the subject.

questions.explanation: "Wrote" is the past tense of the verb "write."We use it when we talk about something someone did already — an action that happened in the past.

questions.subject: English

options.text: I'm just passing away, Ali. Thought I might run into Ahmed here.
options.explanation: Pass away means to die, which is not happening in this sentence. Hence this is incorrect.

options.text: I'm just passing through, Ali. Thought I might run into Ahmed here.
options.explanation: Pass through means to make a journey from one place to another.

options.text: l'm just passing on, Ali. Thought I might run Into Ahmed here.
options.explanation: Pass on means to take or give away something. Hence this is incorrect.

options.text: I'm just passing along, Ali. Thought I might run into Ahmed here.
options.explanation: Pass along means to convey something. So this is incorrect according to the context.

questions.explanation: ‘I'm just passing through, Ali. Thought I might run into Ahmed here.’ Pass-through: a place through which one passes or is obliged to pass.

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D) below each. The milkman _ many bottles of milk to our school every day.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The verb 'delivers' is in the present simple tense, which is appropriate for a routine action. Since 'milkman' is singular, we use 'delivers' to maintain subject-verb agreement.
'Has deliver' is incorrect because the correct form is 'has delivered' for the present perfect tense, which is not used here. The sentence describes a habitual action.
'Have deliver' is also incorrect for the same reasons as 'has deliver'. 'Have' is used with plural subjects, but 'milkman' is singular.
'Delivering' is a present participle, usually used in present continuous tense, which requires a form of 'to be' (e.g., 'is delivering'). It does not fit the simple present tense required here.

questions.explanation: The sentence 'The milkman _ many bottles of milk to our school every day.' requires a verb that agrees with the singular subject 'milkman' and fits the context of a habitual action. The correct answer is 'delivers' because it is in the present simple tense, suitable for describing routine actions. 'Has deliver' and 'have deliver' are incorrect as they misuse the verb forms for the present perfect tense, and 'delivering' is incorrect because it is a participle used in continuous tenses.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:It will _ me a lot of money.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Costs" is present tense and does not fit the future tense context of the sentence.

The correct option that fits appropriately in the blank is:"It will cost me a lot of money."The correct form of the verb "cost" to use in this sentence is the simple present tense "cost" as it indicates a general or future action. The other options are not suitable in this context. "Costs" is also in the present tense, but it is used for third-person singular subjects (e.g., "It costs a lot of money."). "Costed" is incorrect as it is not a standard form of the verb. "Is costing" is in the present progressive tense, which doesn't fit well with the sentence's context.
Costed" is not a standard word in English. The correct past tense of "cost" is also "cost."
"Is costing" is present continuous tense and does not fit the future tense context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: The correct option that fits appropriately in the blank is:"It will cost me a lot of money."The correct form of the verb "cost" to use in this sentence is the simple present tense "cost" as it indicates a general or future action. The other options are not suitable in this context. "Costs" is also in the present tense, but it is used for third-person singular subjects (e.g., "It costs a lot of money."). "Costed" is incorrect as it is not a standard form of the verb. "Is costing" is in the present progressive tense, which doesn't fit well with the sentence's context.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:_ icon like this is called _ hourglass.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An, A — It is wrong because "a" can't be used before "hourglass" (silent 'h').
An, The — "The" would refer to a specific hourglass, but here it's a general statement about what it's called.

This is the correct answer, here's why;Before "icon":"Icon" starts with a vowel sound (i), so we use "an"."An icon like this..."Before "hourglass":Although "hourglass" starts with "h", the 'h' is silent, so it sounds like it starts with a vowel sound (our-glass).So, we use "an" before "hourglass".
The, An — Wrong because "the icon" would refer to a specific one already mentioned, but it's introduced for the first time.

questions.explanation: "An icon like this is called an hourglass."Here's why each article is used:"An" before "icon": The indefinite article "an" is used before "icon" because "icon" starts with a vowel sound."An" before "hourglass": The indefinite article "an" is used before "hourglass" for the same reason — "hourglass" also starts with a vowel sound.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:Plenty of mangoes and bananas _ available in this season.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: - This option is incorrect because the subject "Plenty of mangoes and bananas" is a plural noun phrase, and "is" is a singular verb.- Using "is" would imply that the subject is singular, which doesn't match the plural noun phrase.

- This option is correct because the subject "Plenty of mangoes and bananas" is a plural noun phrase, and "are" is a plural verb.- "Are" agrees with the plural subject and indicates that multiple items (mangoes and bananas) are available.

- This option is incorrect because "was" is a singular past tense verb, and the subject is plural.- Additionally, the sentence is talking about the current season, so the past tense doesn't fit the context.
- This option is incorrect because "were" is a plural past tense verb, but the sentence is talking about the current season, so the past tense doesn't fit the context.

questions.explanation: When deciding whether to use is or are, look at whether the noun is plural or singular. If the noun is singular, use is. If it is plural or there is more than one noun, use are. Were and was is past tense so doesn’t fit.

questions.text: Demonstrate the correct use of subject-verb agreement in the following sentence:The police _ released any information about the case.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: ‘Any information’ demands the use of a negative verb [hasn’t/haven’t] so the Option A and Option B. If there had been ‘all the information’ written in the sentence, then we would have chosen a positive verb [has/have].

‘Any information’ demands the use of a negative verb [hasn’t/haven’t] so the Option A and Option B. If there had been ‘all the information’ written in the sentence, then we would have chosen a positive verb [has/have].
If they had mentioned specific police[e.g. ‘The Punjab police’], then we would have chosen the singular verb [The Punjab police has arrested].
‘The police’ is referring to a general police which is taken as a collective noun so must be followed by a plural negative verb [haven’t]

questions.explanation: ‘Any information’ demands the use of a negative verb [hasn’t/haven’t] so Option A and Option B. If there had been ‘all the information’ written in the sentence, then we would have chosen a positive verb [has/have]. Option C: If they had mentioned specific police[e.g. ‘ The Punjab police’] , then we would have chosen the singular verb [The Punjab police has arrested]Option D: ‘The police’ refers to a general police which is taken as a collective noun so must be followed by a plural negative verb.

options.explanation: "Need" is a plural verb form, incorrect for a singular subject.

Needing is not used as the sentence is not is continuous tense

questions.explanation: The negative form needn't (or need not) is also used to express necessity or obligation; but in the negative sense—i.e., the absence of necessity or obligation to do something.

questions.text: A bouquet of flowers _ required for the event.

options.explanation: This answer choice is incorrect because "are" is the plural form of the verb, and the subject "a bouquet of flowers" is singular.
This answer choice is incorrect because "have" is the plural form of the verb, and the subject "a bouquet of flowers" is singular.

This answer choice is incorrect because "has" is the singular form of the verb used with third-person singular subjects. However, "a bouquet of flowers" is a collective noun and is still considered singular.
As the subject is a singular noun (a bouquet ), the missing word should be 'Is'.

questions.explanation: As the subject is a singular noun (a bouquet ), the missing word should be 'Is'. As the sentence is not in a third person singular, ‘has’ is not used. ‘Are’ is used for plural nouns and ‘have’ is used to express possession, ownership, or acquisition which is not required in this context.

questions.text: Not only, the students but also, their instructor --- been called to the principal's office.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is correct because it treats the compound subject as singular due to the structure 'not only...but also'. The closest subject, 'instructor', is singular, so the verb 'has' is appropriately used.

This option is incorrect because 'have' is a plural verb. The compound subject 'Not only the students but also their instructor' is considered singular for the purposes of verb agreement, thus requiring a singular verb.
This option is incorrect as 'were' is a plural verb. The subject structure indicates a singular subject due to the 'not only...but also' construction, which means the verb should also be singular.

This option is incorrect because 'was' is singular but does not match the intended meaning of the sentence. The correct verb, 'has', aligns with the singular subject, 'instructor', making 'was' an unsuitable choice in this context.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is has because the construction 'Not only...but also' typically treats the closest subject as the primary subject for verb agreement. In this case, 'instructor' is singular, thus requiring the singular verb 'has'. The options 'have' and 'were' are incorrect because they are plural verbs that do not correspond with the singular subject. Although 'was' is singular, it does not fit the context of the sentence as well as 'has', which implies a more immediate action in this scenario.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the correct word. The shepherd ploughed this mountain with cattle the first time it _ ever ploughed.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The sentence is referring to a past event, indicating that the shepherd ploughed the mountain with cattle for the first time. The use of the past tense "was" is appropriate to describe the action that occurred in the past. Therefore, the correct sentence is: "The shephard ploughed this mountain with cattle for the first time it was ever ploughed."

The phrase "was been" is incorrect in this blank because it represents a redundant and grammatically incorrect use of verb forms."Was" and "been" are both past tense forms of the verb "to be," but they cannot be used together in a sentence. The correct forms are either "was" or "been," depending on the context.Here's a breakdown of the correct usage:"Was" is the simple past tense of the verb "to be" and is used when describing an action that happened in the past, without any specific relation to the present. For example: "He was happy yesterday.""Been" is the past participle of the verb "to be" and is used in combination with other verbs to indicate actions that started in the past and continue into the present or have completed at some point before the present. For example: "He has been to London."In the given sentence, "the first time it was ever ploughed," the use of "was" correctly indicates the past action of the mountain being ploughed for the first time. Adding "been" after "was" would create a grammatically incorrect structure, as "been" is not required in this context.Corrected sentence: The shepherd ploughed this mountain with cattle the first time it was ever ploughed.

The word "had" is incorrect in this blank because it represents the past perfect tense, which is not appropriate for this context.The past perfect tense is used to describe an action that occurred before another action in the past. It is formed by combining the past tense of "have" (had) with the past participle of the main verb. For example: "He had already eaten before I arrived."In the original sentence, we are not describing an action that occurred before another past action. Instead, we are simply stating that the mountain was ploughed for the first time. There is no need to introduce a past perfect construction here because there is no other past action in the sentence that the ploughing happened before.

The phrase "had been" is the past perfect continuous tense, which is used to describe an action that started in the past, continued for some time, and then stopped before another past event. It is formed by combining the past perfect tense of "have" (had) with the past participle of the main verb (in this case, "been").

questions.explanation: The sentence is referring to a past event, indicating that the shepherd ploughed the mountain with cattle for the first time. The use of the past tense "was" is appropriate to describe the action that occurred in the past. Therefore, the correct sentence is: "The shephard ploughed this mountain with cattle for the first time it was ever ploughed."

questions.text: Sadia wore her rain boots; _ her feet stayed dry during the storm.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "however" is used to indicate contrast or contradiction. It wouldn't logically connect the first part of the sentence ("Sadia wore her rain boots") with the result that follows ("her feet stayed dry").

This is the correct answer. "Therefore" is used to show a logical consequence or conclusion. In this context, wearing rain boots logically leads to the result that Sadia's feet stayed dry during the storm.

This option is incorrect because "on the other hand" is used to introduce a contrasting point of view or situation, which is not suitable in this sentence where the second clause ("her feet stayed dry") is a direct consequence of the first clause ("Sadia wore her rain boots").
This option is incorrect because "still" is used to indicate continuation or persistence despite other factors. It doesn't logically connect the two parts of the sentence as cause and effect.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. "Therefore" is used to show a logical consequence or conclusion. In this context, wearing rain boots logically leads to the result that Sadia's feet stayed dry during the storm.
